full_notes:
Temple Architecture and Sculpture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that temple architecture in India (Outline map not to scale)

The text explains that t T T T TEMPLE EMPLE EMPLE EMPLE EMPLE A A A A ARCHITECTURE RCHITECTURE RCHITECTURE RCHITECTURE RCHITECTURE AND AND AND AND AND S S S S SCULPTURE CULPTURE CULPTURE CULPTURE CULPTURE 6 Today when we say 'temple' in English we generally mean a devalaya, devkula mandir, kovil, deol, devasthanam or prasada depending on which part of India we are in.

The text explains that mOST of the art and architectural remains that survive from Ancient and Medieval India are religious in nature.

The text explains that that does not mean that people did not have art in their homes at those times, but domestic dwellings and the things in them were mostly made from materials like wood and clay which have perished.

The text explains that this chapter introduces us to many types of temples from India.

The text explains that although we have focussed mostly on Hindu temples, at the end of the chapter you will find some information on major Buddhist and Jain temples too.

The text explains that however, at all times, we must keep in mind that religious shrines were also made for many local cults in villages and forest areas, but again, not being of stone the ancient or medieval shrines in those areas have also vanished.

The text explains that early Temples Early Temples Early Temples Early Temples Early Temples While construction of stupas continued, Brahmanical temples and images of gods also started getting constructed.

The text explains that often temples were decorated with the images of gods.

The text explains that myths mentioned in the Puranas became part of narrative representation of the Brahmanical religion.

The text explains that the shrines of the temples were of three kinds—(i) sandhara type (with pradikshinapatha), (ii) nirandhara type (without pradakshinapatha), and (iii) sarvatobhadra (which can be accessed from all sides).

The text explains that some of the important temple sites of this period are Deogarh in Uttar Chatur Mukhlinga, Nachna- Kuthara (Inset) Shiva temple, Nachna-Kuthara, Madhya Pradesh, fifth century CE

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 70 Kalasha Amalaka Garbhagriha Pitha Shikhara Nagara temple Pradesh, Eran, Nachna-Kuthara and Udaygiri near Vidisha in Madhya Pradesh.

The text explains that these temples are simple structures consisting of a veranda, a hall and a shrine at the rear.

The text explains that tHE BASIC FORM OF THE HINDU TEMPLE The basic form of the Hindu temple comprises the following: (i) sanctum (garbhagriha literally ‘womb-house’), which was a small cubicle with a single entrance and grew into a larger chamber in time.

The text explains that the garbhagriha is made to house the main icon which is itself the focus of much ritual attention; (ii) the entrance to the temple w h i c h m a y b e a p o r t i c o o r c o l o n n a d e d h a l l t h a t incorporates space for a large number of worshippers and is known as a mandapa; (iii) freestanding temples tend to have a mountain-like spire, which can take the shape of a curving shikhar in North India and a pyramidal tower, called a vimana, in South India; (iv) the vahan, i.e., the mount or vehicle of the temple’s main deity along with a standard pillar or dhvaj is placed axially before the sanctum.

The text explains that we will look into the differences in the forms further on in this chapter.

The text explains that sCULPTURE, ICONOGRAPHY AND ORNAMENTATION The study of images of deities falls within a branch of art history called ‘iconography’, which consists of identification of images based on certain symbols and mythologies associated with them.

The text explains that the deities of directions, i.e., the ashtadikpalas face the eight key directions on the outer walls of the sanctum and/or on the outer walls of a temple.

The text explains that the garbhagriha is always located directly under the tallest tower.

The text explains that tEMPLE ARCHITECTURE AND SCULPTURE 73 the ground-plan of many of the Buddhist rock-cut chaitya caves, you will notice that they are shaped as long halls which end in a curved back.

The text explains that this west-facing temple has a grand doorway with standing sculptures of female figures representing the Ganga on the left side and the Yamuna on the right side.

The text explains that in the architecture and the sculptures of this temple, which is a massive structure, we see all features of central Indian temples of the medival period for which they are known and appreciated all over.

The text explains that many Hindu temples, therefore, feature mithun (embracing couple) sculptures, considered auspicious.

The text explains that khajuraho’s sculptures are highly stylised with typical features: they are in almost full relief, cut away from the surrounding stone, with sharp noses, prominent chins, long slanting eyes and eyebrows.

The text explains that proximity of sacred architecture to a water body such as a tank, a river or a pond has been noticed right from the earliest times.

The text explains that however, the walls of the central small shrine are devoid of carving and are left plain as the temple faces the east and, every year, at the time of the equinoxes, the sun shines directly into this central shrine.

The text explains that the history of architecture in the North-East and Bengal is hard to study because a number of ancient buildings in those regions Sun temple, Modhera, Gujarat

The text explains that this feature was eventually even adopted in Mughal buildings, and is known across North India as the Bangla roof.

The text explains that in the Mughal period and later, scores of terracotta brick temples were built across Bengal and Bangladesh in a unique style that had elements of local building techniques seen in bamboo huts which were combined with older forms reminiscent of the Pala period and with the forms of arches and domes that were taken from Islamic architecture.

The text explains that tEMPLE ARCHITECTURE AND SCULPTURE 79 Odisha: The main architectural features of Odisha temples are classified in three orders, i.e., rekhapida, pidhadeul and khakra.

The text explains that the ground plan of the main temple is almost always square, which, in the upper reaches of its superstructure becomes circular in the crowning mastaka.

The text explains that compartments and niches are generally square, the exterior of the temples are lavishly carved, their interiors generally quite bare.

The text explains that the whole temple thus comes to resemble a colossal processional chariot.

The text explains that it is said that there were three such images, each carved out of a different stone placed on the three temple walls, each facing different directions.

The text explains that the outermost is the newest, while the tower right in the centre housing the garbhagriha is the oldest.

The text explains that aN INTRODUCTION TO INDIAN ART 84 The Pallavas were one of the ancient South Indian dynasties that were active in the Andhra region from the second century CE onwards and moved south to settle in Tamil Nadu.

The text explains that their history is better documented from the sixth to the eighth century, when they left many inscriptions in stone and several monuments.

The text explains that although they were mostly Shaivite, several Vaishnava shrines also survived from their reign, and there is no doubt that they were influenced by the long Buddhist history of the Deccan.

The text explains that their early buildings, it is generally assumed, were rock- cut, while the later ones were structural.

The text explains that however, there is reason to believe that structural buildings were well known even when rock-cut ones were being excavated.

The text explains that narasimhavarman I, also known as Mamalla, who acceded the Pallava throne around 640 CE, is celebrated for the expansion of the empire, avenging the defeat his father had suffered at the hands of Pulakesin II, and inaugurating most of the building works at Mahabalipuram which is known after him as Mamallapuram.

The text explains that bigger in scale than anything built by their predecessors, the Pallavas, Chalukyas or Pandyas, this Chola temple’s pyramidal multi-storeyed vimana rises a massive, 70 metre (230 ft. approx) structure topped by a monolithic shikhara which is an octagonal dome-shaped stupika.

The text explains that their greatest achievement in architecture is the Kailashnath temple at Ellora, a culmination of at least a millennium-long tradition in rock-cut architecture in India.

The text explains that importantly, all of this is carved out of living rock.

The text explains that early Chalukyan activity also takes the form of rock-cut caves while later activity is of structural temples.

The text explains that one of the most important sculptures at the site is of Nataraja, surrounded by larger -than-life-size depictions of the saptamatrikas: three to Shiva’s left and four to his right.

The text explains that perhaps the most characteristic feature of these temples is that they grow extremely complex with so many projecting angles emerging from the previously straightforward square temple, that the plan of these temples starts looking like a star, and is thus known as a stellate-plan.

The text explains that so intricate is the carving that it is said, for instance, in the bottom-most frieze featuring a continuous procession of hundreds of elephants with their mahouts, no two elephants are in the same pose.

The text explains that above all, by studying these artworks, we are able to learn much about the kind of society that made these objects.

The text explains that through them we can surmise what their buildings were like, what types of clothes they wore and above all we can use the art material to reconstruct the history of their religions.

The text explains that these religions, as we have seen were many and diverse and constantly changing.

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 6: Temple Architecture and Sculpture
